sections.title: -(으)ㄴ — Past (Verbs) and Present (Adjectives)
sections.explanation: In Korean, relative clauses come BEFORE the noun they modify (the opposite of English). The modifier ending -(으)ㄴ has two uses depending on the word type. With ACTION VERBS, -(으)ㄴ marks PAST tense: 먹다 → 먹은 (that one ate), 만들다 → 만든 (that one made), 읽다 → 읽은 (that one read). Examples: 어제 먹은 음식 (the food I ate yesterday), 내가 만든 케이크 (the cake I made). With DESCRIPTIVE VERBS (adjectives), -(으)ㄴ marks PRESENT state: 크다 → 큰 (big), 작다 → 작은 (small), 예쁘다 → 예쁜 (pretty). Examples: 큰 집 (a big house), 예쁜 꽃 (a pretty flower). The rule: consonant-ending stems take -은, vowel-ending stems take -ㄴ. ㄹ-ending stems drop ㄹ and take -ㄴ: 만들다 → 만든, 길다 → 긴.
sections.tip: Action verbs + -(으)ㄴ = past modifier (the thing someone did). Adjectives + -(으)ㄴ = present modifier (the thing that is). This is because adjectives in Korean describe current states, not actions.

examples.en: The book I read yesterday was interesting.
Please eat the food I made.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the food I ate' using 먹다?
exercises.explanation: For action verbs in past tense, use -(으)ㄴ: 먹다 → 먹은. 먹은 음식 = the food (that I) ate.

exercises.prompt: Which is correct for 'a small room' using 작다 (to be small)?
exercises.explanation: Adjectives use -(으)ㄴ for present-state modification: 작다 → 작은. 작은 방 = a small room.

exercises.prompt: 내가 ___ 영화는 슬펐어요. (The movie I watched was sad.) [보다 = to watch]
exercises.explanation: 보다 (vowel ending) + ㄴ = 본. 내가 본 영화 = the movie I watched.

sections.title: -는 — Present Tense (Action Verbs)
sections.explanation: -는 is the present-tense modifier ending for ACTION VERBS. It describes something happening now or habitually. Attach -는 directly to the verb stem: 먹다 → 먹는 (that is eating / that eats), 가다 → 가는 (that is going / that goes), 읽다 → 읽는 (that is reading / that reads). Examples: 지금 먹는 음식 (the food I'm eating now), 매일 가는 카페 (the cafe I go to every day), 여기서 일하는 사람 (the person who works here). For ㄹ-ending stems, drop ㄹ before -는: 살다 → 사는 (living), 만들다 → 만드는 (making). Important: -는 is ONLY for action verbs. Adjectives do NOT use -는 for present tense — they use -(으)ㄴ instead. This is a key difference from many other languages.
sections.tip: Present action = -는: 먹는 사람 (person who is eating). Present state (adjectives) = -(으)ㄴ: 큰 사람 (big person). Don't mix them up!

examples.en: What is the food you're eating now?
I have a friend who lives in Seoul.
The coffee I drink every day is delicious.
It's the teacher who teaches Korean.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the person who is singing' using 노래하다?
exercises.explanation: Present-tense action verb modifier: 노래하다 → 노래하는. 노래하는 사람 = the person who is singing.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct present modifier for 살다 (to live)? (ㄹ-irregular)
exercises.explanation: ㄹ-ending stems drop ㄹ before -는: 살다 → 사는. 서울에 사는 사람 = a person who lives in Seoul.

exercises.prompt: 여기서 일___ 사람이 많아요. (There are many people who work here.)
exercises.hint: Present modifier ending for action verbs
exercises.explanation: 일하다 + -는 = 일하는. 일하는 사람 = person who works.

sections.title: -(으)ㄹ — Future / Prospective Modifier
sections.explanation: -(으)ㄹ is the future or prospective modifier ending, meaning 'that will' or 'that is to be.' Consonant-ending stems take -을, vowel-ending stems take -ㄹ: 먹다 → 먹을 (that will eat / to be eaten), 가다 → 갈 (that will go), 읽다 → 읽을 (that will read). Examples: 내일 먹을 음식 (the food to eat tomorrow), 읽을 책 (a book to read), 갈 곳 (a place to go). This ending also appears in many grammar patterns: -(으)ㄹ 수 있다 (can do), -(으)ㄹ 거예요 (will do), -(으)ㄹ까요? (shall we?), -(으)ㄹ 때 (when). The prospective meaning often implies purpose or plans: 마실 물 (water to drink), 입을 옷 (clothes to wear). For ㄹ-ending stems, the ㄹ is kept: 만들다 → 만들 (that will make), 살다 → 살 (that will live).
sections.tip: Past -(으)ㄴ, Present -는, Future -(으)ㄹ — these three modifiers are the backbone of Korean relative clauses. Master them and you can describe any noun with a clause.

examples.en: I have a lot of things to do this weekend.
I'm looking for a house to live in.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'a book to read' using 읽다?
exercises.explanation: Future/prospective modifier: 읽다 → 읽을. 읽을 책 = a book to read (that will be read).

exercises.prompt: Which set correctly shows past, present, and future modifiers for 먹다?
exercises.explanation: 먹다: past = 먹은, present = 먹는, future = 먹을. This is the standard tense modifier pattern for action verbs.

exercises.prompt: 내일 ___ 곳이 어디예요? (Where is the place we'll go tomorrow?) [가다 = to go]
exercises.explanation: 가다 (vowel ending) + ㄹ = 갈. 갈 곳 = a place to go.
